Toyuke Daijinja is considered one of the locations associated with the Moto Ise tradition, the original site of the Gekū, and is also known as the "Moto Ise Gekū." The shrine is part of the "Moto Ise Sansha" ({{ill|Moto Ise Three Shrines|ja|元伊勢三社}}) in the Ōe area of Fukuchiyama. The three shrines include: {|class="wikitable" |- ! colspan="3" | Moto Ise Three Shrines in Ōe, Fukuchiyama City |- |'''{{ill|Kotai Shrine|ja|皇大神社 (福知山市)}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Naikū |Origin site of the {{ill|Inner Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|皇大神宮}} (Naikū) |- |'''{{ill|Toyouke Shrine (Fukuchiyama)|ja|豊受大神社|lt=Toyouke Daijinja}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Amatanai |Origin site of the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮}} (Gekū) |- |'''{{ill|Amanoiwato_Shrine_(Fukuchiyama)|ja|天岩戸神社_(福知山市)|lt=Amanoiwato Shrine}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Busshōji |Inner Shrine’s Okumiya |} == Enshrined Deities == ; Main deity * '''{{ill|Toyoukehime-no-Mikoto|ja|豊受姫命}}''' - The deity enshrined at the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮}} (Gekū) ; Other Deities * {{ill|Hikohananinigi-no-Mikoto|ja|日子番能邇邇芸尊}} * {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命}} * {{ill|Futodama-no-Mikoto|ja|天太玉命}} == History == According to the {{ill|Tōyuke-sha Gishiki-chō|ja|止由気宮儀式帳}} (804 CE), Emperor Yūryaku relocated the deity Toyouke from [[Tanba Province]] to [[Ise Province]] upon receiving a divine command from {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} in a dream. This became the Gekū of Ise Grand Shrine. The shrine claims to be the former site of this event. Other accounts suggest the shrine was established en route from [[Hinumanai Shrine|Hino-minai Shrine]] in [[Kyōtango]] during the deity’s journey to Ise ({{ill|Kasa-gun Gazetteer|ja|加佐郡誌}}). Another version links the shrine to {{ill|Prince Maro|ja|麻呂子親王}}, who established it after vanquishing demons in the area ({{ill|Tango Province Historical Records|ja|丹後州宮津府志}}). In the [[Edo period]], the shrine received significant support from the {{ill|Miyazu Domain|ja|宮津藩}}, with the lord {{ill|Kyōgoku Takakuni|ja|京極高国}} funding the construction of the shrine's buildings in 1656 CE. In 1930, it was designated a Prefectural Shrine, and after World War II, it became affiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Grounds == File:Toyuke-daijinja haiden.JPG|'''Haiden and Honden''' (rear)
The honden features [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architecture with a [[thatched roof]], external {{ill|Chigi|ja|千木}}, and {{ill|Katsuogi|ja|鰹木}} on the ridge. Traditionally, it underwent {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮}} every 60 years.Shirakawa Co., Showa 60. The current honden was reconstructed in 1874. File:Toyuke-daijinja torii.JPG|'''Torii''' - A kurokigi (blackwood) torii, similar to {{ill|Nonomiya Shrine|ja|野宮神社}}. * '''{{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}}''' - Features {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|寄棟造}} with shingled roofing. == Subsidiary Shrines == === Sessha (Branch Shrines) === Located around the main hall: * '''Taga Shrine''' - Left of Haiden * '''Tsuchi Shrine''' - Right of Haiden * '''Tsukuyomi-no-Miya''' - Rear left of Honden * '''Kaze-no-Miya''' - Rear right of Honden === Massha (End Shrines) === * Total: 37 shrines :: Arranged in a U-shape around the main hall, each massha was historically maintained by one of the shrine's 37 associated families, which also served as shrine caretakers ({{ill|Oshi|ja|御師}}). [[File:Motoise-gegu02.png|thumb|Diagram of shrine layout.]] == location == ; Address * Ōe-chō, Amatanai, Higashidaira 178-2, [[Fukuchiyama]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] ; Access {{underline|By Train}} * Nearest station: [[Kyoto Tango Railway]] [[Miyafuku Line]] {{ill|Ōe High School-mae Station|ja|大江高校前駅}} (about 15 minutes on foot) {{underline|By Car}} * [[Kyoto Prefectural Route 9|Kyoto Route 9 Ayabe-Ōe-Miyazu Line]] ; Nearby * '''Miyagawa''' - A river near the shrine, sharing its name with {{ill|Miyagawa River (Mie Prefecture)|ja|宮川}} near Ise Grand Shrine. == Notes == {{Reflist|2}} == References == * {{Cite book|author=Association of Shinto Shrines Research Division|title=Jinja Meikan|publisher=Association of Shinto Shrines|date=1963}} * {{Cite book|author=Kenichi Tanigawa|title=Japan’s Gods – Shrines and Sacred Sites|volume=Vol. 7|series=San’in (Revised and Republished)|publisher=Hakusuisha|date=1985|isbn=978-4-560-02217-3}} * {{Cite book|author=Naokazu Miyaji, Ariyoshi Saeki (Supervisors)|title=Shinto Daijiten (Reprint Edition)|publisher=Rinsen Books|date=1969|isbn=978-4-653-01347-0}} == See Also == * {{ill|Hinumanai Shrine|ja|比沼麻奈為神社}} == External It holds the status of a former [[Prefectural shrine]] and is one of the sites connected to the {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=Moto-Ise|ja|元伊勢}} tradition. == Overview == The enshrined deity, {{ill|Toyoukehime-no-Mikoto|ja|豊受姫命}}, was relocated from [[Tanba Province]] to the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮}} (Gekū) during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]], according to legend. Toyuke Daijinja is considered one of the locations associated with the Moto Ise tradition, the original site of the Gekū, and is also known as the "Moto Ise Gekū." The shrine is part of the "Moto Ise Sansha" ({{ill|Moto Ise Three Shrines|ja|元伊勢三社}}) in the Ōe area of Fukuchiyama. The three shrines include: {|class="wikitable" |- ! colspan="3" | Moto Ise Three Shrines in Ōe, Fukuchiyama City |- |'''{{ill|Kotai Shrine|ja|皇大神社 (福知山市)}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Naikū |Origin site of the {{ill|Inner Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|皇大神宮}} (Naikū) |- |'''{{ill|Toyouke Shrine (Fukuchiyama)|ja|豊受大神社|lt=Toyouke Daijinja}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Amatanai |Origin site of the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮}} (Gekū) |- |'''{{ill|Amanoiwato_Shrine_(Fukuchiyama)|ja|天岩戸神社_(福知山市)|lt=Amanoiwato Shrine}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Busshōji |Inner Shrine’s Okumiya |} == Enshrined Deities == ; Main deity * '''{{ill|Toyoukehime-no-Mikoto|ja|豊受姫命}}''' - The deity enshrined at the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮}} (Gekū) ; Other Deities * {{ill|Hikohananinigi-no-Mikoto|ja|日子番能邇邇芸尊}} * {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命}} * {{ill|Futodama-no-Mikoto|ja|天太玉命}} == History == According to the {{ill|Tōyuke-sha Gishiki-chō|ja|止由気宮儀式帳}} (804 CE), Emperor Yūryaku relocated the deity Toyouke from [[Tanba Province]] to [[Ise Province]] upon receiving a divine command from {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} in a dream. Other accounts suggest the shrine was established en route from [[Hinumanai Shrine|Hino-minai Shrine]] in [[Kyōtango]] during the deity’s journey to Ise ({{ill|Kasa-gun Gazetteer|ja|加佐郡誌}}). Another version links the shrine to {{ill|Prince Maro|ja|麻呂子親王}}, who established it after vanquishing demons in the area ({{ill|Tango Province Historical Records|ja|丹後州宮津府志}}). In the [[Edo period]], the shrine received significant support from the {{ill|Miyazu Domain|ja|宮津藩}}, with the lord {{ill|Kyōgoku Takakuni|ja|京極高国}} funding the construction of the shrine's buildings in 1656 CE. In 1930, it was designated a Prefectural Shrine, and after World War II, it became affiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Grounds == File:Toyuke-daijinja haiden.JPG|'''Haiden and Honden''' (rear)
The honden features [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architecture with a [[thatched roof]], external {{ill|Chigi|ja|千木}}, and {{ill|Katsuogi|ja|鰹木}} on the ridge. Traditionally, it underwent {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮}} every 60 years.Shirakawa Co., Showa 60. The current honden was reconstructed in 1874. File:Toyuke-daijinja torii.JPG|'''Torii''' - A kurokigi (blackwood) torii, similar to {{ill|Nonomiya Shrine|ja|野宮神社}}. * '''{{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}}''' - Features {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|寄棟造}} with shingled roofing. == Subsidiary Shrines == === Sessha (Branch Shrines) === Located around the main hall: * '''Taga Shrine''' - Left of Haiden * '''Tsuchi Shrine''' - Right of Haiden * '''Tsukuyomi-no-Miya''' - Rear left of Honden * '''Kaze-no-Miya''' - Rear right of Honden === Massha (End Shrines) === * Total: 37 shrines :: Arranged in a U-shape around the main hall, each massha was historically maintained by one of the shrine's 37 associated families, which also served as shrine caretakers ({{ill|Oshi|ja|御師}}). Toyuke Daijinja is considered one of the locations associated with the Moto Ise tradition, the original site of the Gekū, and is also known as the "Moto Ise Gekū." The shrine is part of the "Moto Ise Sansha" ({{ill|Moto Ise Three Shrines|ja|元伊勢三社}}) in the Ōe area of Fukuchiyama. The three shrines include: {|class="wikitable" |- ! colspan="3" | Moto Ise Three Shrines in Ōe, Fukuchiyama City |- |'''{{ill|Kotai Shrine|ja|皇大神社 (福知山市)}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Naikū |Origin site of the {{ill|Inner Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Naikū) |- |'''{{ill|Toyouke Shrine (Fukuchiyama)|ja|豊受大神社|lt=Toyouke Daijinja}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Amatanai |Origin site of the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮}} (Gekū) |- |'''{{ill|Amanoiwato_Shrine_(Fukuchiyama)|ja|天岩戸神社_(福知山市)|lt=Amanoiwato Shrine}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Busshōji |Inner Shrine’s Okumiya |} == Enshrined Deities == ; Main deity * '''{{ill|Toyoukehime-no-Mikoto|ja|豊受姫命}}''' - The deity enshrined at the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮}} (Gekū) ; Other Deities * {{ill|Hikohananinigi-no-Mikoto|ja|日子番能邇邇芸尊}} * {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命}} * {{ill|Futodama-no-Mikoto|ja|天太玉命}} == History == According to the {{ill|Tōyuke-sha Gishiki-chō|ja|止由気宮儀式帳}} (804 CE), Emperor Yūryaku relocated the deity Toyouke from [[Tanba Province]] to [[Ise Province]] upon receiving a divine command from {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} in a dream. This became the Gekū of Ise Grand Shrine. The shrine claims to be the former site of this event. Other accounts suggest the shrine was established en route from [[Hinumanai Shrine|Hino-minai Shrine]] in [[Kyōtango]] during the deity’s journey to Ise ({{ill|Kasa-gun Gazetteer|ja|加佐郡誌}}). Another version links the shrine to {{ill|Prince Maro|ja|麻呂子親王}}, who established it after vanquishing demons in the area ({{ill|Tango Province Historical Records|ja|丹後州宮津府志}}). In the [[Edo period]], the shrine received significant support from the {{ill|Miyazu Domain|ja|宮津藩}}, with the lord {{ill|Kyōgoku Takakuni|ja|京極高国}} funding the construction of the shrine's buildings in 1656 CE. In 1930, it was designated a Prefectural Shrine, and after World War II, it became affiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Grounds == File:Toyuke-daijinja haiden.JPG|'''Haiden and Honden''' (rear)
The honden features [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architecture with a [[thatched roof]], external {{ill|Chigi|ja|千木}}, and {{ill|Katsuogi|ja|鰹木}} on the ridge. Traditionally, it underwent {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮}} every 60 years.Shirakawa Co., Showa 60. The current honden was reconstructed in 1874. File:Toyuke-daijinja torii.JPG|'''Torii''' - A kurokigi (blackwood) torii, similar to {{ill|Nonomiya Shrine|ja|野宮神社}}. * '''{{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}}''' - Features {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|寄棟造}} with shingled roofing. == Subsidiary Shrines == === Sessha (Branch Shrines) === Located around the main hall: * '''Taga Shrine''' - Left of Haiden * '''Tsuchi Shrine''' - Right of Haiden * '''Tsukuyomi-no-Miya''' - Rear left of Honden * '''Kaze-no-Miya''' - Rear right of Honden === Massha (End Shrines) === * Total: 37 shrines :: Arranged in a U-shape around the main hall, each massha was historically maintained by one of the shrine's 37 associated families, which also served as shrine caretakers ({{ill|Oshi|ja|御師}}). [[File:Motoise-gegu02.png|thumb|Diagram of shrine layout.]] == location == ; Address * Ōe-chō, Amatanai, Higashidaira 178-2, [[Fukuchiyama]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] ; Access {{underline|By Train}} * Nearest station: [[Kyoto Tango Railway]] [[Miyafuku Line]] {{ill|Ōe High School-mae Station|ja|大江高校前駅}} (about 15 minutes on foot) {{underline|By Car}} * [[Kyoto Prefectural Route 9|Kyoto Route 9 Ayabe-Ōe-Miyazu Line]] ; Nearby * '''Miyagawa''' - A river near the shrine, sharing its name with {{ill|Miyagawa River (Mie Prefecture)|ja|宮川}} near Ise Grand Shrine. == Notes == {{Reflist|2}} == References == * {{Cite book|author=Association of Shinto Shrines Research Division|title=Jinja Meikan|publisher=Association of Shinto Shrines|date=1963}} * {{Cite book|author=Kenichi Tanigawa|title=Japan’s Gods – Shrines and Sacred Sites|volume=Vol. 7|series=San’in (Revised and Republished)|publisher=Hakusuisha|date=1985|isbn=978-4-560-02217-3}} * {{Cite book|author=Naokazu Miyaji, Ariyoshi Saeki (Supervisors)|title=Shinto Daijiten (Reprint Edition)|publisher=Rinsen Books|date=1969|isbn=978-4-653-01347-0}} == See Also == * {{ill|Hinumanai Shrine|ja|比沼麻奈為神社}} == External It holds the status of a former [[Prefectural shrine]] and is one of the sites connected to the {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=Moto-Ise|ja|元伊勢}} tradition. == Overview == The enshrined deity, {{ill|Toyoukehime-no-Mikoto|ja|豊受姫命}}, was relocated from [[Tanba Province]] to the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū) during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]], according to legend. Toyuke Daijinja is considered one of the locations associated with the Moto Ise tradition, the original site of the Gekū, and is also known as the "Moto Ise Gekū." The shrine is part of the "Moto Ise Sansha" ({{ill|Moto Ise Three Shrines|ja|元伊勢三社}}) in the Ōe area of Fukuchiyama. The three shrines include: {|class="wikitable" |- ! colspan="3" | Moto Ise Three Shrines in Ōe, Fukuchiyama City |- |'''{{ill|Kotai Shrine|ja|皇大神社 (福知山市)}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Naikū |Origin site of the {{ill|Inner Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Naikū) |- |'''{{ill|Toyouke Shrine (Fukuchiyama)|ja|豊受大神社|lt=Toyouke Daijinja}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Amatanai |Origin site of the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū) |- |'''{{ill|Amanoiwato_Shrine_(Fukuchiyama)|ja|天岩戸神社_(福知山市)|lt=Amanoiwato Shrine}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Busshōji |Inner Shrine’s Okumiya |} == Enshrined Deities == ; Main deity * '''{{ill|Toyoukehime-no-Mikoto|ja|豊受姫命}}''' - The deity enshrined at the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū) ; Other Deities * {{ill|Hikohananinigi-no-Mikoto|ja|日子番能邇邇芸尊}} * {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命}} * {{ill|Futodama-no-Mikoto|ja|天太玉命}} == History == According to the {{ill|Tōyuke-sha Gishiki-chō|ja|止由気宮儀式帳}} (804 CE), Emperor Yūryaku relocated the deity Toyouke from [[Tanba Province]] to [[Ise Province]] upon receiving a divine command from {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} in a dream. This became the Gekū of Ise Grand Shrine. The shrine claims to be the former site of this event. Other accounts suggest the shrine was established en route from [[Hinumanai Shrine|Hino-minai Shrine]] in [[Kyōtango]] during the deity’s journey to Ise ({{ill|Kasa-gun Gazetteer|ja|加佐郡誌}}). Another version links the shrine to {{ill|Prince Maro|ja|麻呂子親王}}, who established it after vanquishing demons in the area ({{ill|Tango Province Historical Records|ja|丹後州宮津府志}}). In the [[Edo period]], the shrine received significant support from the {{ill|Miyazu Domain|ja|宮津藩}}, with the lord {{ill|Kyōgoku Takakuni|ja|京極高国}} funding the construction of the shrine's buildings in 1656 CE. In 1930, it was designated a Prefectural Shrine, and after World War II, it became affiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Grounds == File:Toyuke-daijinja haiden.JPG|'''Haiden and Honden''' (rear)
The honden features [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architecture with a [[thatched roof]], external {{ill|Chigi|ja|千木}}, and {{ill|Katsuogi|ja|鰹木}} on the ridge. Traditionally, it underwent {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮}} every 60 years.Shirakawa Co., Showa 60. The current honden was reconstructed in 1874. File:Toyuke-daijinja torii.JPG|'''Torii''' - A kurokigi (blackwood) torii, similar to {{ill|Nonomiya Shrine|ja|野宮神社}}. * '''{{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}}''' - Features {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|寄棟造}} with shingled roofing. == Subsidiary Shrines == === Sessha (Branch Shrines) === Located around the main hall: * '''Taga Shrine''' - Left of Haiden * '''Tsuchi Shrine''' - Right of Haiden * '''Tsukuyomi-no-Miya''' - Rear left of Honden * '''Kaze-no-Miya''' - Rear right of Honden === Massha (End Shrines) === * Total: 37 shrines :: Arranged in a U-shape around the main hall, each massha was historically maintained by one of the shrine's 37 associated families, which also served as shrine caretakers ({{ill|Oshi|ja|御師}}). It holds the status of a former [[Prefectural shrine]] and is one of the sites connected to the {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=Moto-Ise|ja|元伊勢}} tradition. == Overview == The enshrined deity, {{ill|Toyoukehime-no-Mikoto|ja|豊受姫命}}, was relocated from [[Tanba Province]] to the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū) during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]], according to legend. Toyuke Daijinja is considered one of the locations associated with the Moto Ise tradition, the original site of the Gekū, and is also known as the "Moto Ise Gekū." The shrine is part of the "Moto Ise Sansha" ({{ill|Moto Ise Three Shrines|ja|元伊勢三社}}) in the Ōe area of Fukuchiyama. The three shrines include: {|class="wikitable" |- ! colspan="3" | {{ill|Three Moto-Ise Shrines of Fukuchiyama|ja|元伊勢三社}} |- |'''{{ill|Kotai Shrine|ja|皇大神社 (福知山市)}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Naikū |Origin site of the {{ill|Inner Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Naikū) |- |'''{{ill|Toyouke Shrine (Fukuchiyama)|ja|豊受大神社|lt=Toyouke Daijinja}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Amatanai |Origin site of the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū) |- |'''{{ill|Amanoiwato_Shrine_(Fukuchiyama)|ja|天岩戸神社_(福知山市)|lt=Amanoiwato Shrine}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Busshōji |Inner Shrine’s Okumiya |} == Enshrined Deities == ; Main deity * '''{{ill|Toyoukehime-no-Mikoto|ja|豊受姫命}}''' - The deity enshrined at the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū) ; Other Deities * {{ill|Hikohananinigi-no-Mikoto|ja|日子番能邇邇芸尊}} * {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命}} * {{ill|Futodama-no-Mikoto|ja|天太玉命}} == History == According to the {{ill|Tōyuke-sha Gishiki-chō|ja|止由気宮儀式帳}} (804 CE), Emperor Yūryaku relocated the deity Toyouke from [[Tanba Province]] to [[Ise Province]] upon receiving a divine command from {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} in a dream. This became the Gekū of Ise Grand Shrine. The shrine claims to be the former site of this event. Other accounts suggest the shrine was established en route from [[Hinumanai Shrine|Hino-minai Shrine]] in [[Kyōtango]] during the deity’s journey to Ise ({{ill|Kasa-gun Gazetteer|ja|加佐郡誌}}). Another version links the shrine to {{ill|Prince Maro|ja|麻呂子親王}}, who established it after vanquishing demons in the area ({{ill|Tango Province Historical Records|ja|丹後州宮津府志}}). In the [[Edo period]], the shrine received significant support from the {{ill|Miyazu Domain|ja|宮津藩}}, with the lord {{ill|Kyōgoku Takakuni|ja|京極高国}} funding the construction of the shrine's buildings in 1656 CE. In 1930, it was designated a Prefectural Shrine, and after World War II, it became affiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Grounds == File:Toyuke-daijinja haiden.JPG|'''Haiden and Honden''' (rear)
The honden features [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architecture with a [[thatched roof]], external {{ill|Chigi|ja|千木}}, and {{ill|Katsuogi|ja|鰹木}} on the ridge. Traditionally, it underwent {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮}} every 60 years.Shirakawa Co., Showa 60. The current honden was reconstructed in 1874. File:Toyuke-daijinja torii.JPG|'''Torii''' - A kurokigi (blackwood) torii, similar to {{ill|Nonomiya Shrine|ja|野宮神社}}. * '''{{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}}''' - Features {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|寄棟造}} with shingled roofing. == Subsidiary Shrines == === Sessha (Branch Shrines) === Located around the main hall: * '''Taga Shrine''' - Left of Haiden * '''Tsuchi Shrine''' - Right of Haiden * '''Tsukuyomi-no-Miya''' - Rear left of Honden * '''Kaze-no-Miya''' - Rear right of Honden === Massha (End Shrines) === * Total: 37 shrines :: Arranged in a U-shape around the main hall, each massha was historically maintained by one of the shrine's 37 associated families, which also served as shrine caretakers ({{ill|Oshi|ja|御師}}). [[File:Motoise-gegu02.png|thumb|Diagram of shrine layout.]] == location == ; Address * Ōe-chō, Amatanai, Higashidaira 178-2, [[Fukuchiyama]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] ; Access {{underline|By Train}} * Nearest station: [[Kyoto Tango Railway]] [[Miyafuku Line]] {{ill|Ōe High School-mae Station|ja|大江高校前駅}} (about 15 minutes on foot) {{underline|By Car}} * [[Kyoto Prefectural Route 9|Kyoto Route 9 Ayabe-Ōe-Miyazu Line]] ; Nearby * '''Miyagawa''' - A river near the shrine, sharing its name with {{ill|Miyagawa River (Mie Prefecture)|ja|宮川}} near Ise Grand Shrine. == Notes == {{Reflist|2}} == It holds the status of a former [[Prefectural shrine]] and is one of the sites connected to the {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=Moto-Ise|ja|元伊勢}} tradition. == Overview == The enshrined deity, {{ill|Toyoukehime-no-Mikoto|ja|豊受姫命}}, was relocated from [[Tanba Province]] to the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū) during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]], according to legend. Toyuke Daijinja is considered one of the locations associated with the Moto Ise tradition, the original site of the Gekū, and is also known as the "Moto Ise Gekū." The shrine is part of the "Moto Ise Sansha" ({{ill|Moto Ise Three Shrines|ja|元伊勢三社}}) in the Ōe area of Fukuchiyama. The three shrines include: {|class="wikitable" |- ! colspan="3" | {{ill|Three Moto-Ise Shrines of Fukuchiyama|ja|元伊勢三社}} |- |'''{{ill|Kotai Shrine|ja|皇大神社 (福知山市)}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Naikū |Origin site of the {{ill|Inner Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Naikū) |- |'''{{ill|Toyouke Shrine (Fukuchiyama)|ja|豊受大神社|lt=Toyouke Daijinja}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Amatanai |Origin site of the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū) |- |'''{{ill|Amanoiwato_Shrine_(Fukuchiyama)|ja|天岩戸神社_(福知山市)|lt=Amanoiwato Shrine}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Busshōji |Inner Shrine’s Okumiya |} == Enshrined Deities == ; Main deity * '''{{ill|Toyoukehime-no-Mikoto|ja|豊受姫命}}''' - The deity enshrined at the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū) ; Other Deities * {{ill|Hikohananinigi-no-Mikoto|ja|日子番能邇邇芸尊}} * {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命}} * {{ill|Futodama-no-Mikoto|ja|天太玉命}} == History == According to the {{ill|Tōyuke-sha Gishiki-chō|ja|止由気宮儀式帳}} (804 CE), Emperor Yūryaku relocated the deity Toyouke from [[Tanba Province]] to [[Ise Province]] upon receiving a divine command from {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} in a dream. This became the Gekū of Ise Grand Shrine. The shrine claims to be the former site of this event. Other accounts suggest the shrine was established en route from [[Hinumanai Shrine|Hino-minai Shrine]] in [[Kyōtango]] during the deity’s journey to Ise ({{ill|Kasa-gun Gazetteer|ja|加佐郡誌}}). Another version links the shrine to {{ill|Prince Maro|ja|麻呂子親王}}, who established it after vanquishing demons in the area ({{ill|Tango Province Historical Records|ja|丹後州宮津府志}}). In the [[Edo period]], the shrine received significant support from the {{ill|Miyazu Domain|ja|宮津藩}}, with the lord {{ill|Kyōgoku Takakuni|ja|京極高国}} funding the construction of the shrine's buildings in 1656 CE. In 1930, it was designated a Prefectural Shrine, and after World War II, it became affiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Grounds == File:Toyuke-daijinja haiden.JPG|'''Haiden and Honden''' (rear)
The honden features [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architecture with a [[thatched roof]], external {{ill|Chigi|ja|千木}}, and {{ill|Katsuogi|ja|鰹木}} on the ridge. Traditionally, it underwent {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮}} every 60 years.Shirakawa Co., Showa 60. The current honden was reconstructed in 1874. File:Toyuke-daijinja torii.JPG|'''Torii''' - A kurokigi (blackwood) torii, similar to {{ill|Nonomiya Shrine|ja|野宮神社}}. * '''{{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}}''' - Features {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|寄棟造}} with shingled roofing. == Subsidiary Shrines == === Sessha (Branch Shrines) === Located around the main hall: * '''Taga Shrine''' - Left of Haiden * '''Tsuchi Shrine''' - Right of Haiden * '''Tsukuyomi-no-Miya''' - Rear left of Honden * '''Kaze-no-Miya''' - Rear right of Honden === Massha (End Shrines) === * Total: 37 shrines :: Arranged in a U-shape around the main hall, each massha was historically maintained by one of the shrine's 37 associated families, which also served as shrine caretakers ({{ill|Oshi|ja|御師}}). [[File:Motoise-gegu02.png|thumb|Diagram of shrine layout.]] == location == ; Address * Ōe-chō, Amatanai, Higashidaira 178-2, [[Fukuchiyama]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] ; Access {{underline|By Train}} * Nearest station: [[Kyoto Tango Railway]] [[Miyafuku Line]] {{ill|Ōe High School-mae Station|ja|大江高校前駅}} (about 15 minutes on foot) {{underline|By Car}} * [[Kyoto Prefectural Route 9|Kyoto Route 9 Ayabe-Ōe-Miyazu Line]] ; Nearby * '''Miyagawa''' - A river near the shrine, sharing its name with {{ill|Miyagawa River (Mie Prefecture)|ja|宮川}} near Ise Grand Shrine. == Notes == {{Reflist|2}} == Haiden (center), Taga Shrine (left), and Tsuchi Shrine (right) |location = [[Kyoto Prefecture]][[Fukuchiyama]]-shi, Ōe-chō, Amatanai, Higashidaira 178-2 |coordinates = {{Coord|35|24|09.2|N|135|09|05.0|E|region:JP|name=Toyuke Daijinja}} |deity = {{ill|Toyoukehime-no-Mikoto|ja|豊受姫命}} |Rank = Former [[Prefectural shrine]] |consecration_year = (Trad.) [[Emperor Yūryaku]], 22nd year |Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style, [[thatched roof]] |Other names = Moto Ise Gekū |Festivals = [[April 26]] |Rituals = Hassaku Festival ([[September 1]]) }} '''Toyuke Daijinja''' ({{ill|豊受大神社|ja|豊受大神社}}) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Ōe]], [[Fukuchiyama]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. It holds the status of a former [[Prefectural shrine]] and is one of the sites connected to the {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=Moto-Ise|ja|元伊勢}} tradition. == Overview == The enshrined deity, {{ill|Toyoukehime-no-Mikoto|ja|豊受姫命}}, was relocated from [[Tanba Province]] to the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū) during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]], according to legend. Toyuke Daijinja is considered one of the locations associated with the Moto Ise tradition, the original site of the Gekū, and is also known as the "Moto Ise Gekū." The shrine is part of the "Moto Ise Sansha" ({{ill|Moto Ise Three Shrines|ja|元伊勢三社}}) in the Ōe area of Fukuchiyama. The three shrines include: {|class="wikitable" |- ! colspan="3" | {{ill|Three Moto-Ise Shrines of Fukuchiyama|ja|元伊勢三社}} |- |'''{{ill|Kotai Shrine|ja|皇大神社 (福知山市)}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Naikū |Origin site of the {{ill|Inner Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Naikū) |- |'''{{ill|Toyouke Shrine (Fukuchiyama)|ja|豊受大神社|lt=Toyouke Daijinja}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Amatanai |Origin site of the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū) |- |'''{{ill|Amanoiwato_Shrine_(Fukuchiyama)|ja|天岩戸神社_(福知山市)|lt=Amanoiwato Shrine}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Busshōji |Inner Shrine’s Okumiya |} == Enshrined Deities == ; Main deity * '''{{ill|Toyoukehime-no-Mikoto|ja|豊受姫命}}''' - The deity enshrined at the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū) ; Other Deities * {{ill|Hikohananinigi-no-Mikoto|ja|日子番能邇邇芸尊}} * {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命}} * {{ill|Futodama-no-Mikoto|ja|天太玉命}} == History == According to the {{ill|Tōyuke-sha Gishiki-chō|ja|止由気宮儀式帳}} (804 CE), Emperor Yūryaku relocated the deity Toyouke from [[Tanba Province]] to [[Ise Province]] upon receiving a divine command from {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} in a dream. This became the Gekū of Ise Grand Shrine. The shrine claims to be the former site of this event. Other accounts suggest the shrine was established en route from [[Hinumanai Shrine|Hino-minai Shrine]] in [[Kyōtango]] during the deity’s journey to Ise ({{ill|Kasa-gun Gazetteer|ja|加佐郡誌}}). Another version links the shrine to {{ill|Prince Maro|ja|麻呂子親王}}, who established it after vanquishing demons in the area ({{ill|Tango Province Historical Records|ja|丹後州宮津府志}}). In the [[Edo period]], the shrine received significant support from the {{ill|Miyazu Domain|ja|宮津藩}}, with the lord {{ill|Kyōgoku Takakuni|ja|京極高国}} funding the construction of the shrine's buildings in 1656 CE. In 1930, it was designated a Prefectural Shrine, and after World War II, it became affiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Grounds == File:Toyuke-daijinja haiden.JPG|'''Haiden and Honden''' (rear)
The honden features [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architecture with a [[thatched roof]], external {{ill|Chigi|ja|千木}}, and {{ill|Katsuogi|ja|鰹木}} on the ridge. Traditionally, it underwent {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮}} every 60 years.Shirakawa Co., Showa 60. The current honden was reconstructed in 1874. File:Toyuke-daijinja torii.JPG|'''Torii''' - A kurokigi (blackwood) torii, similar to {{ill|Nonomiya Shrine|ja|野宮神社}}. * '''{{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}}''' - Features {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|寄棟造}} with shingled roofing. == Subsidiary Shrines == === Sessha (Branch Shrines) === Located around the main hall: * '''Taga Shrine''' - Left of Haiden * '''Tsuchi Shrine''' - Right of Haiden * '''Tsukuyomi-no-Miya''' - Rear left of Honden * '''Kaze-no-Miya''' - Rear right of Honden === Massha (End Shrines) === * Total: 37 shrines :: Arranged in a U-shape around the main hall, each massha was historically maintained by one of the shrine's 37 associated families, which also served as shrine caretakers ({{ill|Oshi|ja|御師}}). [[File:Motoise-gegu02.png|thumb|Diagram of shrine layout.]] == location == ; Address * Ōe-chō, Amatanai, Higashidaira 178-2, [[Fukuchiyama]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] ; Access {{underline|By Train}} * Nearest station: [[Kyoto Tango Railway]] [[Miyafuku Line]] {{ill|Ōe High School-mae Station|ja|大江高校前駅}} (about 15 minutes on foot) {{underline|By Car}} * [[Kyoto Prefectural Route 9|Kyoto Route 9 Ayabe-Ōe-Miyazu Line]] ; Nearby * '''Miyagawa''' - A river near the shrine, sharing its name with {{ill|Miyagawa River (Mie Prefecture)|ja|宮川}} near Ise Grand Shrine. == Notes == {{Reflist|2}} == In the [[Edo period]], the shrine received significant support from the {{ill|Miyazu Domain|ja|宮津藩}}, with the lord {{ill|Kyōgoku Takakuni|ja|京極高国}} funding the construction of the shrine's buildings in 1656 CE. The honden features [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architecture with a [[thatched roof]], external {{ill|Chigi|ja|千木}}, and {{ill|Katsuogi|ja|鰹木}} on the ridge. Traditionally, it underwent {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮}} every 60 years.Shirakawa Co., Showa 60. The current honden was reconstructed in 1874. File:Toyuke-daijinja torii.JPG|'''Torii''' - A kurokigi (blackwood) torii, similar to {{ill|Nonomiya Shrine|ja|野宮神社}}. * '''{{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}}''' - Features {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|寄棟造}} with shingled roofing. == Subsidiary Shrines == === Sessha (Branch Shrines) === Located around the main hall: * '''Taga Shrine''' - Left of Haiden * '''Tsuchi Shrine''' - Right of Haiden * '''Tsukuyomi-no-Miya''' - Rear left of Honden * '''Kaze-no-Miya''' - Rear right of Honden === Massha (End Shrines) === * Total: 37 shrines :: Arranged in a U-shape around the main hall, each massha was historically maintained by one of the shrine's 37 associated families, which also served as shrine caretakers ({{ill|Oshi|ja|御師}}). [[File:Motoise-gegu02.png|thumb|Diagram of shrine layout.]] == location == ; Address * Ōe-chō, Amatanai, Higashidaira 178-2, [[Fukuchiyama]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] ; Access {{underline|By Train}} * Nearest station: [[Kyoto Tango Railway]] [[Miyafuku Line]] {{ill|Ōe High School-mae Station|ja|大江高校前駅}} (about 15 minutes on foot) {{underline|By Car}} * [[Kyoto Prefectural Route 9|Kyoto Route 9 Ayabe-Ōe-Miyazu Line]] ; Nearby * '''Miyagawa''' - A river near the shrine, sharing its name with {{ill|Miyagawa River (Mie Prefecture)|ja|宮川}} near Ise Grand Shrine. == Notes == {{Reflist|2}} == References == * {{Cite book|author=Association of Shinto Shrines Research Division|title=Jinja Meikan|publisher=Association of Shinto Shrines|date=1963}} * {{Cite book|author=Kenichi Tanigawa|title=Japan’s Gods – Shrines and Sacred Sites|volume=Vol. 7|series=San’in (Revised and Republished)|publisher=Hakusuisha|date=1985|isbn=978-4-560-02217-3}} * {{Cite book|author=Naokazu Miyaji, Ariyoshi Saeki (Supervisors)|title=Shinto Daijiten (Reprint Edition)|publisher=Rinsen Books|date=1969|isbn=978-4-653-01347-0}} == See Also == * {{ill|Hinumanai Shrine|ja|比沼麻奈為神社}} == External ```wiki {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Toyuke Daijinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Toyukedaijinja shrine.jpg|280px]]
Haiden (center), Taga Shrine (left), and Tsuchi Shrine (right) |location = [[Kyoto Prefecture]][[Fukuchiyama]]-shi, Ōe-chō, Amatanai, Higashidaira 178-2 |coordinates = {{Coord|35|24|09.2|N|135|09|05.0|E|region:JP|name=Toyuke Daijinja}} |deity = {{ill|Toyoukehime-no-Mikoto|ja|豊受姫命}} |Rank = Former [[Prefectural shrine]] |consecration_year = (Trad.) [[Emperor Yūryaku]], 22nd year |Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style, [[thatched roof]] |Other names = Moto Ise Gekū |Festivals = [[April 26]] |Rituals = Hassaku Festival ([[September 1]]) }} '''Toyuke Daijinja''' ({{ill|豊受大神社|ja|豊受大神社}}) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Ōe]], [[Fukuchiyama]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. It holds the status of a former [[Prefectural shrine]] and is one of the sites connected to the {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=Moto-Ise|ja|元伊勢}} tradition. == Overview == The enshrined deity, {{ill|Toyoukehime-no-Mikoto|ja|豊受姫命}}, was relocated from [[Tanba Province]] to the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū) during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]], according to legend. Toyuke Daijinja is considered one of the locations associated with the Moto Ise tradition, the original site of the Gekū, and is also known as the "Moto Ise Gekū." The shrine is part of the "Moto Ise Sansha" ({{ill|Moto Ise Three Shrines|ja|元伊勢三社}}) in the Ōe area of Fukuchiyama. The three shrines include: {|class="wikitable" |- ! colspan="3" | {{ill|Three Moto-Ise Shrines of Fukuchiyama|ja|元伊勢三社}} |- |'''{{ill|Kotai Shrine|ja|皇大神社 (福知山市)}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Naikū |Origin site of the {{ill|Inner Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Naikū) |- |'''{{ill|Toyouke Shrine (Fukuchiyama)|ja|豊受大神社|lt=Toyouke Daijinja}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Amatanai |Origin site of the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū) |- |'''{{ill|Amanoiwato_Shrine_(Fukuchiyama)|ja|天岩戸神社_(福知山市)|lt=Amanoiwato Shrine}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Busshōji |Inner Shrine’s Okumiya |} == Enshrined Deities == ; Main deity * '''{{ill|Toyoukehime-no-Mikoto|ja|豊受姫命}}''' - The deity enshrined at the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū) ; Other Deities * {{ill|Hikohananinigi-no-Mikoto|ja|日子番能邇邇芸尊}} * {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命}} * {{ill|Futodama-no-Mikoto|ja|天太玉命}} == History == According to the {{ill|Tōyuke-sha Gishiki-chō|ja|止由気宮儀式帳}} (804 CE), Emperor Yūryaku relocated the deity Toyouke from [[Tanba Province]] to [[Ise Province]] upon receiving a divine command from {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} in a dream. This became the Gekū of Ise Grand Shrine. The shrine claims to be the former site of this event. Other accounts suggest the shrine was established en route from [[Hinumanai Shrine|Hino-minai Shrine]] in [[Kyōtango]] during the deity’s journey to Ise ({{ill|Kasa-gun Gazetteer|ja|加佐郡誌}}). Another version links the shrine to {{ill|Prince Maro|ja|麻呂子親王}}, who established it after vanquishing demons in the area ({{ill|Tango Province Historical Records|ja|丹後州宮津府志}}). In the [[Edo period]], the shrine received significant support from the {{ill|Miyazu Domain|ja|宮津藩}}, with the lord {{ill|Kyōgoku Takakuni|ja|京極高国}} funding the construction of the shrine's buildings in 1656 CE. In 1930, it was designated a Prefectural Shrine, and after World War II, it became affiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Grounds == File:Toyuke-daijinja haiden.JPG|'''Haiden and Honden''' (rear)
The honden features [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architecture with a [[thatched roof]], external {{ill|Chigi|ja|千木}}, and {{ill|Katsuogi|ja|鰹木}} on the ridge. Traditionally, it underwent {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮}} every 60 years.Shirakawa Co., Showa 60. The current honden was reconstructed in 1874. File:Toyuke-daijinja torii.JPG|'''Torii''' - A kurokigi (blackwood) torii, similar to {{ill|Nonomiya Shrine|ja|野宮神社}}. * '''{{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}}''' - Features {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|寄棟造}} with shingled roofing. == Subsidiary Shrines == === Sessha (Branch Shrines) === Located around the main hall: * '''Taga Shrine''' - Left of Haiden * '''Tsuchi Shrine''' - Right of Haiden * '''Tsukuyomi-no-Miya''' - Rear left of Honden * '''Kaze-no-Miya''' - Rear right of Honden === Massha (End Shrines) === * Total: 37 shrines :: Arranged in a U-shape around the main hall, each massha was historically maintained by one of the shrine's 37 associated families, which also served as shrine caretakers ({{ill|Oshi|ja|御師}}). [[File:Motoise-gegu02.png|thumb|Diagram of shrine layout.]] == location == ; Address * Ōe-chō, Amatanai, Higashidaira 178-2, [[Fukuchiyama]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] ; Access {{underline|By Train}} * Nearest station: [[Kyoto Tango Railway]] [[Miyafuku Line]] {{ill|Ōe High School-mae Station|ja|大江高校前駅}} (about 15 minutes on foot) {{underline|By Car}} * [[Kyoto Prefectural Route 9|Kyoto Route 9 Ayabe-Ōe-Miyazu Line]] ; Nearby * '''Miyagawa''' - A river near the shrine, sharing its name with {{ill|Miyagawa River (Mie Prefecture)|ja|宮川}} near Ise Grand Shrine. == Notes == {{Reflist|2}} == References == * {{Cite book|author=Association of Shinto Shrines Research Division|title=Jinja Meikan|publisher=Association of Shinto Shrines|date=1963}} * {{Cite book|author=Kenichi Tanigawa|title=Japan’s Gods – Shrines and Sacred Sites|volume=Vol. 7|series=San’in (Revised and Republished)|publisher=Hakusuisha|date=1985|isbn=978-4-560-02217-3}} * {{Cite book|author=Naokazu Miyaji, Ariyoshi Saeki (Supervisors)|title=Shinto Daijiten (Reprint Edition)|publisher=Rinsen Books|date=1969|isbn=978-4-653-01347-0}} == See Also == * {{ill|Hinumanai Shrine|ja|比沼麻奈為神社}} == External The honden features [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architecture with a [[thatched roof]], external {{ill|Chigi|ja|千木}}, and {{ill|Katsuogi|ja|鰹木}} on the ridge. Traditionally, it underwent {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮}} every 60 years.Shirakawa Co., Showa 60. The current honden was reconstructed in 1874. File:Toyuke-daijinja torii.JPG|'''Torii''' - A kurokigi (blackwood) torii, similar to {{ill|Nonomiya Shrine|ja|野宮神社}}. * '''{{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}}''' - Features {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|寄棟造}} with shingled roofing. == Subsidiary Shrines == === Sessha (Branch Shrines) === Located around the main hall: * '''Taga Shrine''' - Left of Haiden * '''Tsuchi Shrine''' - Right of Haiden * '''Tsukuyomi-no-Miya''' - Rear left of Honden * '''Kaze-no-Miya''' - Rear right of Honden === Massha (End Shrines) === * Total: 37 shrines :: Arranged in a U-shape around the main hall, each massha was historically maintained by one of the shrine's 37 associated families, which also served as shrine caretakers ({{ill|Oshi|ja|御師}}). [[File:Motoise-gegu02.png|thumb|Diagram of shrine layout.]] == location == ; Address * Ōe-chō, Amatanai, Higashidaira 178-2, [[Fukuchiyama]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] ; Access {{underline|By Train}} * Nearest station: [[Kyoto Tango Railway]] [[Miyafuku Line]] {{ill|Ōe High School-mae Station|ja|大江高校前駅}} (about 15 minutes on foot) {{underline|By Car}} * [[Kyoto Prefectural Route 9|Kyoto Route 9 Ayabe-Ōe-Miyazu Line]] ; Nearby * '''Miyagawa''' - A river near the shrine, sharing its name with {{ill|Miyagawa River (Mie Prefecture)|ja|宮川}} near Ise Grand Shrine. == Notes == {{Reflist|2}} == Tanigawa|title=Japan’s Gods – Shrines and Sacred Sites|volume=Vol. 7|series=San’in (Revised and Republished)|publisher=Hakusuisha|date=1985|isbn=978-4-560-02217-3}} * {{Cite book|author=Naokazu Miyaji, Ariyoshi Saeki (Supervisors)|title=Shinto Daijiten (Reprint Edition)|publisher=Rinsen Books|date=1969|isbn=978-4-653-01347-0}} == See Also == * {{ill|Hinumanai Shrine|ja|比沼麻奈為神社}} == External ```wiki {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Toyuke Daijinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Toyukedaijinja shrine.jpg|280px]]
Haiden (center), Taga Shrine (left), and Tsuchi Shrine (right) |location = [[Kyoto Prefecture]][[Fukuchiyama]]-shi, Ōe-chō, Amatanai, Higashidaira 178-2 |coordinates = {{Coord|35|24|09.2|N|135|09|05.0|E|region:JP|name=Toyuke Daijinja}} |deity = {{ill|Toyoukehime-no-Mikoto|ja|豊受姫命}} |Rank = Former [[Prefectural shrine]] |consecration_year = (Trad.) [[Emperor Yūryaku]], 22nd year |Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style, [[thatched roof]] |Other names = Moto Ise Gekū |Festivals = [[April 26]] |Rituals = Hassaku Festival ([[September 1]]) }} '''Toyuke Daijinja''' ({{ill|豊受大神社|ja|豊受大神社}}) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Ōe]], [[Fukuchiyama]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. It holds the status of a former [[Prefectural shrine]] and is one of the sites connected to the {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=Moto-Ise|ja|元伊勢}} tradition. == Overview == The enshrined deity, {{ill|Toyoukehime-no-Mikoto|ja|豊受姫命}}, was relocated from [[Tanba Province]] to the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū) during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]], according to legend. Toyuke Daijinja is considered one of the locations associated with the Moto Ise tradition, the original site of the Gekū, and is also known as the "Moto Ise Gekū." The shrine is part of the "Moto Ise Sansha" ({{ill|Moto Ise Three Shrines|ja|元伊勢三社}}) in the Ōe area of Fukuchiyama. The three shrines include: {|class="wikitable" |- ! colspan="3" | {{ill|Three Moto-Ise Shrines of Fukuchiyama|ja|元伊勢三社}} |- |'''{{ill|Kotai Shrine|ja|皇大神社 (福知山市)}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Naikū |Origin site of the {{ill|Inner Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Naikū) |- |'''{{ill|Toyouke Shrine (Fukuchiyama)|ja|豊受大神社|lt=Toyouke Daijinja}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Amatanai |Origin site of the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū) |- |'''{{ill|Amanoiwato_Shrine_(Fukuchiyama)|ja|天岩戸神社_(福知山市)|lt=Amanoiwato Shrine}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Busshōji |Inner Shrine’s Okumiya |} == Enshrined Deities == ; Main deity * '''{{ill|Toyoukehime-no-Mikoto|ja|豊受姫命}}''' - The deity enshrined at the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū) ; Other Deities * {{ill|Hikohananinigi-no-Mikoto|ja|日子番能邇邇芸尊}} * {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命}} * {{ill|Futodama-no-Mikoto|ja|天太玉命}} == History == According to the {{ill|Tōyuke-sha Gishiki-chō|ja|止由気宮儀式帳}} (804 CE), Emperor Yūryaku relocated the deity Toyouke from [[Tanba Province]] to [[Ise Province]] upon receiving a divine command from {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} in a dream. This became the Gekū of Ise Grand Shrine. The shrine claims to be the former site of this event. Other accounts suggest the shrine was established en route from [[Hinumanai Shrine|Hino-minai Shrine]] in [[Kyōtango]] during the deity’s journey to Ise ({{ill|Kasa-gun Gazetteer|ja|加佐郡誌}}). Another version links the shrine to {{ill|Prince Maro|ja|麻呂子親王}}, who established it after vanquishing demons in the area ({{ill|Tango Province Historical Records|ja|丹後州宮津府志}}). In the [[Edo period]], the shrine received significant support from the {{ill|Miyazu Domain|ja|宮津藩}}, with the lord {{ill|Kyōgoku Takakuni|ja|京極高国}} funding the construction of the shrine's buildings in 1656 CE. In 1930, it was designated a Prefectural Shrine, and after World War II, it became affiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Grounds == File:Toyuke-daijinja haiden.JPG|'''Haiden and Honden''' (rear)
The honden features [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architecture with a [[thatched roof]], external {{ill|Chigi|ja|千木}}, and {{ill|Katsuogi|ja|鰹木}} on the ridge. Traditionally, it underwent {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮}} every 60 years.Shirakawa Co., Showa 60. The current honden was reconstructed in 1874. File:Toyuke-daijinja torii.JPG|'''Torii''' - A kurokigi (blackwood) torii, similar to {{ill|Nonomiya Shrine|ja|野宮神社}}. * '''{{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}}''' - Features {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|寄棟造}} with shingled roofing. == Subsidiary Shrines == === Sessha (Branch Shrines) === Located around the main hall: * '''Taga Shrine''' - Left of Haiden * '''Tsuchi Shrine''' - Right of Haiden * '''Tsukuyomi-no-Miya''' - Rear left of Honden * '''Kaze-no-Miya''' - Rear right of Honden === Massha (End Shrines) === * Total: 37 shrines :: Arranged in a U-shape around the main hall, each massha was historically maintained by one of the shrine's 37 associated families, which also served as shrine caretakers ({{ill|Oshi|ja|御師}}). [[File:Motoise-gegu02.png|thumb|Diagram of shrine layout.]] == location == ; Address * Ōe-chō, Amatanai, Higashidaira 178-2, [[Fukuchiyama]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] ; Access {{underline|By Train}} * Nearest station: [[Kyoto Tango Railway]] [[Miyafuku Line]] {{ill|Ōe High School-mae Station|ja|大江高校前駅}} (about 15 minutes on foot) {{underline|By Car}} * [[Kyoto Prefectural Route 9|Kyoto Route 9 Ayabe-Ōe-Miyazu Line]] ; Nearby * '''Miyagawa''' - A river near the shrine, sharing its name with {{ill|Miyagawa River (Mie Prefecture)|ja|宮川}} near Ise Grand Shrine. == Notes == {{Reflist|2}} == References == * {{Cite book|author=Association of Shinto Shrines Research Division|title=Jinja Meikan|publisher=Association of Shinto Shrines|date=1963}} * {{Cite book|author=Kenichi Tanigawa|title=Japan’s Gods – Shrines and Sacred Sites|volume=Vol. 7|series=San’in (Revised and Republished)|publisher=Hakusuisha|date=1985|isbn=978-4-560-02217-3}} * {{Cite book|author=Naokazu Miyaji, Ariyoshi Saeki (Supervisors)|title=Shinto Daijiten (Reprint Edition)|publisher=Rinsen Books|date=1969|isbn=978-4-653-01347-0}} == See Also == * {{ill|Hinumanai Shrine|ja|比沼麻奈為神社}} == External Kōtaijinja Official Site - Moto Ise Three Shrines] * [https://www.uminokyoto.jp/genryu/fukuchiyama/page4.html Moto Ise Three Shrines - Fukuchiyama - Sea of Kyoto (Northern Kyoto Tourism Office)] The honden features [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architecture with a [[thatched roof]], external {{ill|Chigi|ja|千木}}, and {{ill|Katsuogi|ja|鰹木}} on the ridge. Traditionally, it underwent {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮}} every 60 years.Shirakawa Co., Showa 60. The current honden was reconstructed in 1874. File:Toyuke-daijinja torii.JPG|'''Torii''' - A kurokigi (blackwood) torii, similar to {{ill|Nonomiya Shrine|ja|野宮神社}}. * '''{{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}}''' - Features {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|寄棟造}} with shingled roofing. == Subsidiary Shrines == === Sessha (Branch Shrines) === Located around the main hall: * '''Taga Shrine''' - Left of Haiden * '''Tsuchi Shrine''' - Right of Haiden * '''Tsukuyomi-no-Miya''' - Rear left of Honden * '''Kaze-no-Miya''' - Rear right of Honden === Massha (End Shrines) === * Total: 37 shrines :: Arranged in a U-shape around the main hall, each massha was historically maintained by one of the shrine's 37 associated families, which also served as shrine caretakers ({{ill|Oshi|ja|御師}}). [[File:Motoise-gegu02.png|thumb|Diagram of shrine layout.]] == location == ; Address * Ōe-chō, Amatanai, Higashidaira 178-2, [[Fukuchiyama]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] ; Access {{underline|By Train}} * Nearest station: [[Kyoto Tango Railway]] [[Miyafuku Line]] {{ill|Ōe High School-mae Station|ja|大江高校前駅}} (about 15 minutes on foot) {{underline|By Car}} * [[Kyoto Prefectural Route 9|Kyoto Route 9 Ayabe-Ōe-Miyazu Line]] ; Nearby * '''Miyagawa''' - A river near the shrine, sharing its name with {{ill|Miyagawa River (Mie Prefecture)|ja|宮川}} near Ise Grand Shrine. == Notes == {{Reflist|2}} == References == * {{Cite book|author=Association of Shinto Shrines Research Division|title=Jinja Meikan|publisher=Association of Shinto Shrines|date=1963}} * {{Cite book|author=Kenichi Tanigawa|title=Japan’s Gods – Shrines and Sacred Sites|volume=Vol. 7|series=San’in (Revised and Republished)|publisher=Hakusuisha|date=1985|isbn=978-4-560-02217-3}} * {{Cite book|author=Naokazu Miyaji, Ariyoshi Saeki (Supervisors)|title=Shinto Daijiten (Reprint Edition)|publisher=Rinsen Books|date=1969|isbn=978-4-653-01347-0}} == See Also == * {{ill|Hinumanai Shrine|ja|比沼麻奈為神社}} == External revision.text:
```wiki {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Toyuke Daijinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Toyukedaijinja shrine.jpg|280px]]
Haiden (center), Taga Shrine (left), and Tsuchi Shrine (right) |location = [[Kyoto Prefecture]][[Fukuchiyama]]-shi, Ōe-chō, Amatanai, Higashidaira 178-2 |coordinates = {{Coord|35|24|09.2|N|135|09|05.0|E|region:JP|name=Toyuke Daijinja}} |deity = {{ill|Toyoukehime-no-Mikoto|ja|豊受姫命}} |Rank = Former [[Prefectural shrine]] |consecration_year = (Trad.) [[Emperor Yūryaku]], 22nd year |Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style, [[thatched roof]] |Other names = Moto Ise Gekū |Festivals = [[April 26]] |Rituals = Hassaku Festival ([[September 1]]) }} '''Toyuke Daijinja''' ({{ill|豊受大神社|ja|豊受大神社}}) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Ōe]], [[Fukuchiyama]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. It holds the status of a former [[Prefectural shrine]] and is one of the sites connected to the {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=Moto-Ise|ja|元伊勢}} tradition. == Overview == The enshrined deity, {{ill|Toyoukehime-no-Mikoto|ja|豊受姫命}}, was relocated from [[Tanba Province]] to the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū) during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]], according to legend. Toyuke Daijinja is considered one of the locations associated with the Moto Ise tradition, the original site of the Gekū, and is also known as the "Moto Ise Gekū." The shrine is part of the "Moto Ise Sansha" ({{ill|Moto Ise Three Shrines|ja|元伊勢三社}}) in the Ōe area of Fukuchiyama. The three shrines include: {|class="wikitable" |- ! colspan="3" | {{ill|Three Moto-Ise Shrines of Fukuchiyama|ja|元伊勢三社}} |- |'''{{ill|Kotai Shrine|ja|皇大神社 (福知山市)}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Naikū |Origin site of the {{ill|Inner Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Naikū) |- |'''{{ill|Toyouke Shrine (Fukuchiyama)|ja|豊受大神社|lt=Toyouke Daijinja}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Amatanai |Origin site of the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū) |- |'''{{ill|Amanoiwato_Shrine_(Fukuchiyama)|ja|天岩戸神社_(福知山市)|lt=Amanoiwato Shrine}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Busshōji |Inner Shrine’s Okumiya |} == Enshrined Deities == ; Main deity * '''{{ill|Toyoukehime-no-Mikoto|ja|豊受姫命}}''' - The deity enshrined at the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū) ; Other Deities * {{ill|Hikohananinigi-no-Mikoto|ja|日子番能邇邇芸尊}} * {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命}} * {{ill|Futodama-no-Mikoto|ja|天太玉命}} == History == According to the {{ill|Tōyuke-sha Gishiki-chō|ja|止由気宮儀式帳}} (804 CE), Emperor Yūryaku relocated the deity Toyouke from [[Tanba Province]] to [[Ise Province]] upon receiving a divine command from {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} in a dream. This became the Gekū of Ise Grand Shrine. The shrine claims to be the former site of this event. Other accounts suggest the shrine was established en route from [[Hinumanai Shrine|Hino-minai Shrine]] in [[Kyōtango]] during the deity’s journey to Ise ({{ill|Kasa-gun Gazetteer|ja|加佐郡誌}}). Another version links the shrine to {{ill|Prince Maro|ja|麻呂子親王}}, who established it after vanquishing demons in the area ({{ill|Tango Province Historical Records|ja|丹後州宮津府志}}). In the [[Edo period]], the shrine received significant support from the {{ill|Miyazu Domain|ja|宮津藩}}, with the lord {{ill|Kyōgoku Takakuni|ja|京極高国}} funding the construction of the shrine's buildings in 1656 CE. In 1930, it was designated a Prefectural Shrine, and after World War II, it became affiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Grounds == File:Toyuke-daijinja haiden.JPG|'''Haiden and Honden''' (rear)
The honden features [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architecture with a [[thatched roof]], external {{ill|Chigi|ja|千木}}, and {{ill|Katsuogi|ja|鰹木}} on the ridge. Traditionally, it underwent {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮}} every 60 years.Shirakawa Co., Showa 60. The current honden was reconstructed in 1874. File:Toyuke-daijinja torii.JPG|'''Torii''' - A kurokigi (blackwood) torii, similar to {{ill|Nonomiya Shrine|ja|野宮神社}}. * '''{{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}}''' - Features {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|寄棟造}} with shingled roofing. == Subsidiary Shrines == === Sessha (Branch Shrines) === Located around the main hall: * '''Taga Shrine''' - Left of Haiden * '''Tsuchi Shrine''' - Right of Haiden * '''Tsukuyomi-no-Miya''' - Rear left of Honden * '''Kaze-no-Miya''' - Rear right of Honden === Massha (End Shrines) === * Total: 37 shrines :: Arranged in a U-shape around the main hall, each massha was historically maintained by one of the shrine's 37 associated families, which also served as shrine caretakers ({{ill|Oshi|ja|御師}}). [[File:Motoise-gegu02.png|thumb|Diagram of shrine layout.]] == location == ; Address * Ōe-chō, Amatanai, Higashidaira 178-2, [[Fukuchiyama]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] ; Access {{underline|By Train}} * Nearest station: [[Kyoto Tango Railway]] [[Miyafuku Line]] {{ill|Ōe High School-mae Station|ja|大江高校前駅}} (about 15 minutes on foot) {{underline|By Car}} * [[Kyoto Prefectural Route 9|Kyoto Route 9 Ayabe-Ōe-Miyazu Line]] ; Nearby * '''Miyagawa''' - A river near the shrine, sharing its name with {{ill|Miyagawa River (Mie Prefecture)|ja|宮川}} near Ise Grand Shrine. == Notes == {{Reflist|2}} == References == * {{Cite book|author=Association of Shinto Shrines Research Division|title=Jinja Meikan|publisher=Association of Shinto Shrines|date=1963}} * {{Cite book|author=Kenichi Tanigawa|title=Japan’s Gods – Shrines and Sacred Sites|volume=Vol. 7|series=San’in (Revised and Republished)|publisher=Hakusuisha|date=1985|isbn=978-4-560-02217-3}} * {{Cite book|author=Naokazu Miyaji, Ariyoshi Saeki (Supervisors)|title=Shinto Daijiten (Reprint Edition)|publisher=Rinsen Books|date=1969|isbn=978-4-653-01347-0}} == See Also == * {{ill|Hinumanai Shrine|ja|比沼麻奈為神社}} == External Links == [[Category:nowiki former opening]]{{Commons|Category:original pages]][[Category:nowiki former closing]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Toyouke Shrine (Fukuchiyama)]][[Category:qq]] [[category:qq close]] [[Category:Toyouke Shrine (Fukuchiyama)]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Toyo'uke Daijingu Shrine}}[[Category:nowiki former opening]][[Category:Toyuke-daijinja}}[[Category:nowiki former closing]] * [https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/65/1115.html Moto Ise Gekū Toyuke Daijinja - Fukuchiyama City Official Homepage] * [https://www.kyoto-kankou.or.jp/info_search/?id=6222&r=1632663304.3013 Moto Ise Gekū Toyuke Daijinja - Kyoto Prefectural Tourism Guide] * [http://motoise-naiku.com/motoise3sya.html Moto Ise Naikū Kōtaijinja Official Site - Moto Ise Three Shrines] * [https://www.uminokyoto.jp/genryu/fukuchiyama/page4.html Moto Ise Three Shrines - Fukuchiyama - Sea of Kyoto (Northern Kyoto Tourism Office)] This became the Gekū of Ise Grand Shrine. The shrine claims to be the former site of this event. Other accounts suggest the shrine was established en route from [[Hinumanai Shrine|Hino-minai Shrine]] in [[Kyōtango]] during the deity’s journey to Ise ({{ill|Kasa-gun Gazetteer|ja|加佐郡誌}}). In 1930, it was designated a Prefectural Shrine, and after World War II, it became affiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Grounds == File:Toyuke-daijinja haiden.JPG|'''Haiden and Honden''' (rear)
The honden features [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architecture with a [[thatched roof]], external {{ill|Chigi|ja|千木}}, and {{ill|Katsuogi|ja|鰹木}} on the ridge. Traditionally, it underwent {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮}} every 60 years.Shirakawa Co., Showa 60. The current honden was reconstructed in 1874. File:Toyuke-daijinja torii.JPG|'''Torii''' - A kurokigi (blackwood) torii, similar to {{ill|Nonomiya Shrine|ja|野宮神社}}. * '''{{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}}''' - Features {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|寄棟造}} with shingled roofing. == Subsidiary Shrines == === Sessha (Branch Shrines) === Located around the main hall: * '''Taga Shrine''' - Left of Haiden * '''Tsuchi Shrine''' - Right of Haiden * '''Tsukuyomi-no-Miya''' - Rear left of Honden * '''Kaze-no-Miya''' - Rear right of Honden === Massha (End Shrines) === * Total: 37 shrines :: Arranged in a U-shape around the main hall, each massha was historically maintained by one of the shrine's 37 associated families, which also served as shrine caretakers ({{ill|Oshi|ja|御師}}). Ayabe-Ōe-Miyazu Line]] ; Nearby * '''Miyagawa''' - A river near the shrine, sharing its name with {{ill|Miyagawa River (Mie Prefecture)|ja|宮川}} near Ise Grand Shrine. == Notes == {{Reflist|2}} == ```wiki {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Toyuke Daijinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Toyukedaijinja shrine.jpg|280px]]
Haiden (center), Taga Shrine (left), and Tsuchi Shrine (right) |location = [[Kyoto Prefecture]][[Fukuchiyama]]-shi, Ōe-chō, Amatanai, Higashidaira 178-2 |coordinates = {{Coord|35|24|09.2|N|135|09|05.0|E|region:JP|name=Toyuke Daijinja}} |deity = {{ill|Toyoukehime-no-Mikoto|ja|豊受姫命}} |Rank = Former [[Prefectural shrine]] |consecration_year = (Trad.) [[Emperor Yūryaku]], 22nd year |Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style, [[thatched roof]] |Other names = Moto Ise Gekū |Festivals = [[April 26]] |Rituals = Hassaku Festival ([[September 1]]) }} '''Toyuke Daijinja''' ({{ill|豊受大神社|ja|豊受大神社}}) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Ōe]], [[Fukuchiyama]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. It holds the status of a former [[Prefectural shrine]] and is one of the sites connected to the {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=Moto-Ise|ja|元伊勢}} tradition. == Overview == The enshrined deity, {{ill|Toyoukehime-no-Mikoto|ja|豊受姫命}}, was relocated from [[Tanba Province]] to the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū) during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]], according to legend. Toyuke Daijinja is considered one of the locations associated with the Moto Ise tradition, the original site of the Gekū, and is also known as the "Moto Ise Gekū." The shrine is part of the "Moto Ise Sansha" ({{ill|Moto Ise Three Shrines|ja|元伊勢三社}}) in the Ōe area of Fukuchiyama. The three shrines include: {|class="wikitable" |- ! colspan="3" | {{ill|Three Moto-Ise Shrines of Fukuchiyama|ja|元伊勢三社}} |- |'''{{ill|Kotai Shrine|ja|皇大神社 (福知山市)}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Naikū |Origin site of the {{ill|Inner Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Naikū) |- |'''{{ill|Toyouke Shrine (Fukuchiyama)|ja|豊受大神社|lt=Toyouke Daijinja}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Amatanai |Origin site of the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū) |- |'''{{ill|Amanoiwato_Shrine_(Fukuchiyama)|ja|天岩戸神社_(福知山市)|lt=Amanoiwato Shrine}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Busshōji |Inner Shrine’s Okumiya |} == Enshrined Deities == ; Main deity * '''{{ill|Toyoukehime-no-Mikoto|ja|豊受姫命}}''' - The deity enshrined at the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū) ; Other Deities * {{ill|Hikohananinigi-no-Mikoto|ja|日子番能邇邇芸尊}} * {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命}} * {{ill|Futodama-no-Mikoto|ja|天太玉命}} == History == According to the {{ill|Tōyuke-sha Gishiki-chō|ja|止由気宮儀式帳}} (804 CE), Emperor Yūryaku relocated the deity Toyouke from [[Tanba Province]] to [[Ise Province]] upon receiving a divine command from {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} in a dream. This became the Gekū of Ise Grand Shrine. The shrine claims to be the former site of this event. Other accounts suggest the shrine was established en route from [[Hinumanai Shrine|Hino-minai Shrine]] in [[Kyōtango]] during the deity’s journey to Ise ({{ill|Kasa-gun Gazetteer|ja|加佐郡誌}}). Another version links the shrine to {{ill|Prince Maro|ja|麻呂子親王}}, who established it after vanquishing demons in the area ({{ill|Tango Province Historical Records|ja|丹後州宮津府志}}). In the [[Edo period]], the shrine received significant support from the {{ill|Miyazu Domain|ja|宮津藩}}, with the lord {{ill|Kyōgoku Takakuni|ja|京極高国}} funding the construction of the shrine's buildings in 1656 CE. In 1930, it was designated a Prefectural Shrine, and after World War II, it became affiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Grounds == File:Toyuke-daijinja haiden.JPG|'''Haiden and Honden''' (rear)
The honden features [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architecture with a [[thatched roof]], external {{ill|Chigi|ja|千木}}, and {{ill|Katsuogi|ja|鰹木}} on the ridge. Traditionally, it underwent {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮}} every 60 years.Shirakawa Co., Showa 60. The current honden was reconstructed in 1874. File:Toyuke-daijinja torii.JPG|'''Torii''' - A kurokigi (blackwood) torii, similar to {{ill|Nonomiya Shrine|ja|野宮神社}}. * '''{{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}}''' - Features {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|寄棟造}} with shingled roofing. == Subsidiary Shrines == === Sessha (Branch Shrines) === Located around the main hall: * '''Taga Shrine''' - Left of Haiden * '''Tsuchi Shrine''' - Right of Haiden * '''Tsukuyomi-no-Miya''' - Rear left of Honden * '''Kaze-no-Miya''' - Rear right of Honden === Massha (End Shrines) === * Total: 37 shrines :: Arranged in a U-shape around the main hall, each massha was historically maintained by one of the shrine's 37 associated families, which also served as shrine caretakers ({{ill|Oshi|ja|御師}}). [[File:Motoise-gegu02.png|thumb|Diagram of shrine layout.]] == location == ; Address * Ōe-chō, Amatanai, Higashidaira 178-2, [[Fukuchiyama]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] ; Access {{underline|By Train}} * Nearest station: [[Kyoto Tango Railway]] [[Miyafuku Line]] {{ill|Ōe High School-mae Station|ja|大江高校前駅}} (about 15 minutes on foot) {{underline|By Car}} * [[Kyoto Prefectural Route 9|Kyoto Route 9 Ayabe-Ōe-Miyazu Line]] ; Nearby * '''Miyagawa''' - A river near the shrine, sharing its name with {{ill|Miyagawa River (Mie Prefecture)|ja|宮川}} near Ise Grand Shrine. == Notes == {{Reflist|2}} == References == * {{Cite book|author=Association of Shinto Shrines Research Division|title=Jinja Meikan|publisher=Association of Shinto Shrines|date=1963}} * {{Cite book|author=Kenichi Tanigawa|title=Japan’s Gods – Shrines and Sacred Sites|volume=Vol. 7|series=San’in (Revised and Republished)|publisher=Hakusuisha|date=1985|isbn=978-4-560-02217-3}} * {{Cite book|author=Naokazu Miyaji, Ariyoshi Saeki (Supervisors)|title=Shinto Daijiten (Reprint Edition)|publisher=Rinsen Books|date=1969|isbn=978-4-653-01347-0}} == See Also == * {{ill|Hinumanai Shrine|ja|比沼麻奈為神社}} == External Moto Ise Gekū Toyuke Daijinja - Kyoto Prefectural Tourism Guide] * [http://motoise-naiku.com/motoise3sya.html Moto Ise Naikū Kōtaijinja Official Site - Moto Ise Three Shrines] * Another version links the shrine to {{ill|Prince Maro|ja|麻呂子親王}}, who established it after vanquishing demons in the area ({{ill|Tango Province Historical Records|ja|丹後州宮津府志}}). In the [[Edo period]], the shrine received significant support from the {{ill|Miyazu Domain|ja|宮津藩}}, with the lord {{ill|Kyōgoku Takakuni|ja|京極高国}} funding the construction of the shrine's buildings in 1656 CE. In 1930, it was designated a Prefectural Shrine, and after World War II, it became affiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Grounds == File:Toyuke-daijinja haiden.JPG|'''Haiden and Honden''' (rear)
The honden features [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architecture with a [[thatched roof]], external {{ill|Chigi|ja|千木}}, and {{ill|Katsuogi|ja|鰹木}} on the ridge. Traditionally, it underwent {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮}} every 60 years.Shirakawa Co., Showa 60. The current honden was reconstructed in 1874. File:Toyuke-daijinja torii.JPG|'''Torii''' - A kurokigi (blackwood) torii, similar to {{ill|Nonomiya Shrine|ja|野宮神社}}. * '''{{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}}''' - Features {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|寄棟造}} with shingled roofing. == Subsidiary Shrines == === Sessha (Branch Shrines) === Located around the main hall: * '''Taga Shrine''' - Left of Haiden * '''Tsuchi Shrine''' - Right of Haiden * '''Tsukuyomi-no-Miya''' - Rear left of Honden * '''Kaze-no-Miya''' - Rear right of Honden === Massha (End Shrines) === * Total: 37 shrines :: Arranged in a U-shape around the main hall, each massha was historically maintained by one of the shrine's 37 associated families, which also served as shrine caretakers ({{ill|Oshi|ja|御師}}). Nearest station: [[Kyoto Tango Railway]] [[Miyafuku Line]] {{ill|Ōe High School-mae Station|ja|大江高校前駅}} (about 15 minutes on foot) {{underline|By Car}} * [[Kyoto Prefectural Route 9|Kyoto Route 9 Ayabe-Ōe-Miyazu Line]] ; Nearby * '''Miyagawa''' - A river near the shrine, sharing its name with {{ill|Miyagawa River (Mie Prefecture)|ja|宮川}} near Ise Grand Shrine. == Notes == {{Reflist|2}} == ```wiki {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Toyuke Daijinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Toyukedaijinja shrine.jpg|280px]]
Haiden (center), Taga Shrine (left), and Tsuchi Shrine (right) |location = [[Kyoto Prefecture]][[Fukuchiyama]]-shi, Ōe-chō, Amatanai, Higashidaira 178-2 |coordinates = {{Coord|35|24|09.2|N|135|09|05.0|E|region:JP|name=Toyuke Daijinja}} |deity = {{ill|Toyoukehime-no-Mikoto|ja|豊受姫命}} |Rank = Former [[Prefectural shrine]] |consecration_year = (Trad.) [[Emperor Yūryaku]], 22nd year |Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style, [[thatched roof]] |Other names = Moto Ise Gekū |Festivals = [[April 26]] |Rituals = Hassaku Festival ([[September 1]]) }} '''Toyuke Daijinja''' ({{ill|豊受大神社|ja|豊受大神社}}) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Ōe]], [[Fukuchiyama]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. It holds the status of a former [[Prefectural shrine]] and is one of the sites connected to the {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=Moto-Ise|ja|元伊勢}} tradition. == Overview == The enshrined deity, {{ill|Toyoukehime-no-Mikoto|ja|豊受姫命}}, was relocated from [[Tanba Province]] to the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū) during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]], according to legend. Toyuke Daijinja is considered one of the locations associated with the Moto Ise tradition, the original site of the Gekū, and is also known as the "Moto Ise Gekū." The shrine is part of the "Moto Ise Sansha" ({{ill|Moto Ise Three Shrines|ja|元伊勢三社}}) in the Ōe area of Fukuchiyama. The three shrines include: {|class="wikitable" |- ! colspan="3" | {{ill|Three Moto-Ise Shrines of Fukuchiyama|ja|元伊勢三社}} |- |'''{{ill|Kotai Shrine|ja|皇大神社 (福知山市)}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Naikū |Origin site of the {{ill|Inner Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Naikū) |- |'''{{ill|Toyouke Shrine (Fukuchiyama)|ja|豊受大神社|lt=Toyouke Daijinja}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Amatanai |Origin site of the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū) |- |'''{{ill|Amanoiwato_Shrine_(Fukuchiyama)|ja|天岩戸神社_(福知山市)|lt=Amanoiwato Shrine}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Busshōji |Inner Shrine’s Okumiya |} == Enshrined Deities == ; Main deity * '''{{ill|Toyoukehime-no-Mikoto|ja|豊受姫命}}''' - The deity enshrined at the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū) ; Other Deities * {{ill|Hikohananinigi-no-Mikoto|ja|日子番能邇邇芸尊}} * {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命}} * {{ill|Futodama-no-Mikoto|ja|天太玉命}} == History == According to the {{ill|Tōyuke-sha Gishiki-chō|ja|止由気宮儀式帳}} (804 CE), Emperor Yūryaku relocated the deity Toyouke from [[Tanba Province]] to [[Ise Province]] upon receiving a divine command from {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} in a dream. This became the Gekū of Ise Grand Shrine. The shrine claims to be the former site of this event. Other accounts suggest the shrine was established en route from [[Hinumanai Shrine|Hino-minai Shrine]] in [[Kyōtango]] during the deity’s journey to Ise ({{ill|Kasa-gun Gazetteer|ja|加佐郡誌}}). Another version links the shrine to {{ill|Prince Maro|ja|麻呂子親王}}, who established it after vanquishing demons in the area ({{ill|Tango Province Historical Records|ja|丹後州宮津府志}}). In the [[Edo period]], the shrine received significant support from the {{ill|Miyazu Domain|ja|宮津藩}}, with the lord {{ill|Kyōgoku Takakuni|ja|京極高国}} funding the construction of the shrine's buildings in 1656 CE. In 1930, it was designated a Prefectural Shrine, and after World War II, it became affiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Grounds == File:Toyuke-daijinja haiden.JPG|'''Haiden and Honden''' (rear)
The honden features [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architecture with a [[thatched roof]], external {{ill|Chigi|ja|千木}}, and {{ill|Katsuogi|ja|鰹木}} on the ridge. Traditionally, it underwent {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮}} every 60 years.Shirakawa Co., Showa 60. The current honden was reconstructed in 1874. File:Toyuke-daijinja torii.JPG|'''Torii''' - A kurokigi (blackwood) torii, similar to {{ill|Nonomiya Shrine|ja|野宮神社}}. * '''{{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}}''' - Features {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|寄棟造}} with shingled roofing. == Subsidiary Shrines == === Sessha (Branch Shrines) === Located around the main hall: * '''Taga Shrine''' - Left of Haiden * '''Tsuchi Shrine''' - Right of Haiden * '''Tsukuyomi-no-Miya''' - Rear left of Honden * '''Kaze-no-Miya''' - Rear right of Honden === Massha (End Shrines) === * Total: 37 shrines :: Arranged in a U-shape around the main hall, each massha was historically maintained by one of the shrine's 37 associated families, which also served as shrine caretakers ({{ill|Oshi|ja|御師}}). [[File:Motoise-gegu02.png|thumb|Diagram of shrine layout.]] == location == ; Address * Ōe-chō, Amatanai, Higashidaira 178-2, [[Fukuchiyama]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] ; Access {{underline|By Train}} * Nearest station: [[Kyoto Tango Railway]] [[Miyafuku Line]] {{ill|Ōe High School-mae Station|ja|大江高校前駅}} (about 15 minutes on foot) {{underline|By Car}} * [[Kyoto Prefectural Route 9|Kyoto Route 9 Ayabe-Ōe-Miyazu Line]] ; Nearby * '''Miyagawa''' - A river near the shrine, sharing its name with {{ill|Miyagawa River (Mie Prefecture)|ja|宮川}} near Ise Grand Shrine. == Notes == {{Reflist|2}} == Other accounts suggest the shrine was established en route from [[Hinumanai Shrine|Hino-minai Shrine]] in [[Kyōtango]] during the deity’s journey to Ise ({{ill|Kasa-gun Gazetteer|ja|加佐郡誌}}). Another version links the shrine to {{ill|Prince Maro|ja|麻呂子親王}}, who established it after vanquishing demons in the area ({{ill|Tango Province Historical Records|ja|丹後州宮津府志}}). In the [[Edo period]], the shrine received significant support from the {{ill|Miyazu Domain|ja|宮津藩}}, with the lord {{ill|Kyōgoku Takakuni|ja|京極高国}} funding the construction of the shrine's buildings in 1656 CE. In 1930, it was designated a Prefectural Shrine, and after World War II, it became affiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Grounds == File:Toyuke-daijinja haiden.JPG|'''Haiden and Honden''' (rear)
The honden features [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architecture with a [[thatched roof]], external {{ill|Chigi|ja|千木}}, and {{ill|Katsuogi|ja|鰹木}} on the ridge. Traditionally, it underwent {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮}} every 60 years.Shirakawa Co., Showa 60. The current honden was reconstructed in 1874. File:Toyuke-daijinja torii.JPG|'''Torii''' - A kurokigi (blackwood) torii, similar to {{ill|Nonomiya Shrine|ja|野宮神社}}. * '''{{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}}''' - Features {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|寄棟造}} with shingled roofing. == Subsidiary Shrines == === Sessha (Branch Shrines) === Located around the main hall: * '''Taga Shrine''' - Left of Haiden * '''Tsuchi Shrine''' - Right of Haiden * '''Tsukuyomi-no-Miya''' - Rear left of Honden * '''Kaze-no-Miya''' - Rear right of Honden === Massha (End Shrines) === * Total: 37 shrines :: Arranged in a U-shape around the main hall, each massha was historically maintained by one of the shrine's 37 associated families, which also served as shrine caretakers ({{ill|Oshi|ja|御師}}). ```wiki {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Toyuke Daijinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Toyukedaijinja shrine.jpg|280px]]
Haiden (center), Taga Shrine (left), and Tsuchi Shrine (right) |location = [[Kyoto Prefecture]][[Fukuchiyama]]-shi, Ōe-chō, Amatanai, Higashidaira 178-2 |coordinates = {{Coord|35|24|09.2|N|135|09|05.0|E|region:JP|name=Toyuke Daijinja}} |deity = {{ill|Toyoukehime-no-Mikoto|ja|豊受姫命}} |Rank = Former [[Prefectural shrine]] |consecration_year = (Trad.) [[Emperor Yūryaku]], 22nd year |Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style, [[thatched roof]] |Other names = Moto Ise Gekū |Festivals = [[April 26]] |Rituals = Hassaku Festival ([[September 1]]) }} '''Toyuke Daijinja''' ({{ill|豊受大神社|ja|豊受大神社}}) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Ōe]], [[Fukuchiyama]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. It holds the status of a former [[Prefectural shrine]] and is one of the sites connected to the {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=Moto-Ise|ja|元伊勢}} tradition. == Overview == The enshrined deity, {{ill|Toyoukehime-no-Mikoto|ja|豊受姫命}}, was relocated from [[Tanba Province]] to the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū) during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]], according to legend. Toyuke Daijinja is considered one of the locations associated with the Moto Ise tradition, the original site of the Gekū, and is also known as the "Moto Ise Gekū." The shrine is part of the "Moto Ise Sansha" ({{ill|Moto Ise Three Shrines|ja|元伊勢三社}}) in the Ōe area of Fukuchiyama. The three shrines include: {|class="wikitable" |- ! colspan="3" | {{ill|Three Moto-Ise Shrines of Fukuchiyama|ja|元伊勢三社}} |- |'''{{ill|Kotai Shrine|ja|皇大神社 (福知山市)}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Naikū |Origin site of the {{ill|Inner Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Naikū) |- |'''{{ill|Toyouke Shrine (Fukuchiyama)|ja|豊受大神社|lt=Toyouke Daijinja}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Amatanai |Origin site of the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū) |- |'''{{ill|Amanoiwato_Shrine_(Fukuchiyama)|ja|天岩戸神社_(福知山市)|lt=Amanoiwato Shrine}}''' |Fukuchiyama, Ōe-chō, Busshōji |Inner Shrine’s Okumiya |} == Enshrined Deities == ; Main deity * '''{{ill|Toyoukehime-no-Mikoto|ja|豊受姫命}}''' - The deity enshrined at the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū) ; Other Deities * {{ill|Hikohananinigi-no-Mikoto|ja|日子番能邇邇芸尊}} * {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命}} * {{ill|Futodama-no-Mikoto|ja|天太玉命}} == History == According to the {{ill|Tōyuke-sha Gishiki-chō|ja|止由気宮儀式帳}} (804 CE), Emperor Yūryaku relocated the deity Toyouke from [[Tanba Province]] to [[Ise Province]] upon receiving a divine command from {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} in a dream. This became the Gekū of Ise Grand Shrine. The shrine claims to be the former site of this event. Other accounts suggest the shrine was established en route from [[Hinumanai Shrine|Hino-minai Shrine]] in [[Kyōtango]] during the deity’s journey to Ise ({{ill|Kasa-gun Gazetteer|ja|加佐郡誌}}). Another version links the shrine to {{ill|Prince Maro|ja|麻呂子親王}}, who established it after vanquishing demons in the area ({{ill|Tango Province Historical Records|ja|丹後州宮津府志}}). In the [[Edo period]], the shrine received significant support from the {{ill|Miyazu Domain|ja|宮津藩}}, with the lord {{ill|Kyōgoku Takakuni|ja|京極高国}} funding the construction of the shrine's buildings in 1656 CE. In 1930, it was designated a Prefectural Shrine, and after World War II, it became affiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Grounds == File:Toyuke-daijinja haiden.JPG|'''Haiden and Honden''' (rear)
The honden features [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architecture with a [[thatched roof]], external {{ill|Chigi|ja|千木}}, and {{ill|Katsuogi|ja|鰹木}} on the ridge. Traditionally, it underwent {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮}} every 60 years.Shirakawa Co., Showa 60. The current honden was reconstructed in 1874. File:Toyuke-daijinja torii.JPG|'''Torii''' - A kurokigi (blackwood) torii, similar to {{ill|Nonomiya Shrine|ja|野宮神社}}. * '''{{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}}''' - Features {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|寄棟造}} with shingled roofing. == Subsidiary Shrines == === Sessha (Branch Shrines) === Located around the main hall: * '''Taga Shrine''' - Left of Haiden * '''Tsuchi Shrine''' - Right of Haiden * '''Tsukuyomi-no-Miya''' - Rear left of Honden * '''Kaze-no-Miya''' - Rear right of Honden === Massha (End Shrines) === * Total: 37 shrines :: Arranged in a U-shape around the main hall, each massha was historically maintained by one of the shrine's 37 associated families, which also served as shrine caretakers ({{ill|Oshi|ja|御師}}). Nearest station: [[Kyoto Tango Railway]] [[Miyafuku Line]] {{ill|Ōe High School-mae Station|ja|大江高校前駅}} (about 15 minutes on foot) {{underline|By Car}} * [[Kyoto Prefectural Route 9|Kyoto Route 9 Ayabe-Ōe-Miyazu Line]] ; Nearby * '''Miyagawa''' - A river near the shrine, sharing its name with {{ill|Miyagawa River (Mie Prefecture)|ja|宮川}} near Ise Grand Shrine. == Notes == {{Reflist|2}} == In 1930, it was designated a Prefectural Shrine, and after World War II, it became affiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Grounds == File:Toyuke-daijinja haiden.JPG|'''Haiden and Honden''' (rear)
The honden features [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architecture with a [[thatched roof]], external {{ill|Chigi|ja|千木}}, and {{ill|Katsuogi|ja|鰹木}} on the ridge. Traditionally, it underwent {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮}} every 60 years.Shirakawa Co., Showa 60. The current honden was reconstructed in 1874. File:Toyuke-daijinja torii.JPG|'''Torii''' - A kurokigi (blackwood) torii, similar to {{ill|Nonomiya Shrine|ja|野宮神社}}. * '''{{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}}''' - Features {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|寄棟造}} with shingled roofing. == Subsidiary Shrines == === Sessha (Branch Shrines) === Located around the main hall: * '''Taga Shrine''' - Left of Haiden * '''Tsuchi Shrine''' - Right of Haiden * '''Tsukuyomi-no-Miya''' - Rear left of Honden * '''Kaze-no-Miya''' - Rear right of Honden === Massha (End Shrines) === * Total: 37 shrines :: Arranged in a U-shape around the main hall, each massha was historically maintained by one of the shrine's 37 associated families, which also served as shrine caretakers ({{ill|Oshi|ja|御師}}). Ayabe-Ōe-Miyazu Line]] ; Nearby * '''Miyagawa''' - A river near the shrine, sharing its name with {{ill|Miyagawa River (Mie Prefecture)|ja|宮川}} near Ise Grand Shrine. == Notes == {{Reflist|2}} ==
